item.title: Block Trade King Simon Sadler Begins High-Profile Court Showdown
item.description: The crowd at Bloomfield Road Stadium chanted Simon Sadler’s name, giving the financier a hero’s welcome.

item.title: Asia Bond Sales Jump in April as War Lull Spurs Rush in Issuance
item.description: Asia-Pacific’s dollar bond market saw its strongest April in five years as the start of a ceasefire in the Iran war opened the floodgates to a rush of issuance.

item.title: Gold Steady With Focus on Hormuz Reopening, Iran Peace Talks
item.description: Gold was little changed as traders monitored President Donald Trump’s plan to start guiding some ships through the Strait of Hormuz and progress toward a US-Iran deal.

item.title: Aussie Rally May Fade as RBA Set to Temper Hikes, Analysts Say
item.description: The Australian dollar’s rally may fade with the Reserve Bank of Australia set to signal a slower pace of interest rate hikes after a widely expected increase this week, according to top forecasters.

item.title: US Futures Gain, Oil Falls on Signs of Iran Talks: Markets Wrap
item.description: US stock futures rose and oil fell as improving risk sentiment was buoyed by prospects of shipping resuming through the Strait of Hormuz and progress toward a US-Iran deal.

item.title: NAB Profit Misses Estimates as Software Costs Weigh on Bank
item.description: National Australia Bank Ltd. missed first-half profit estimates as robust loan growth was offset by higher software costs and credit provisions as the economy deteriorates.

item.title: Oil Falls as Trump Says US to Start Guiding Ships Through Hormuz
item.description: Oil dropped as President Donald Trump said the US would begin guiding neutral ships through the Strait of Hormuz and signaled progress on talks with Iran to end the war.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Reignites Trump’s War on Disney
item.description: Brendan Carr doesn’t need Disney to cancel Jimmy Kimmel to declare victory

item.title: A2 Milk Recalls Batches of US Baby Formula After Detecting Toxin
item.description: New Zealand infant formula supplier a2 Milk is recalling batches of product sold in the US after detecting the toxin cereulide.

item.title: Trillions in Retirement Dollars Flow Into Opaque Trusts
item.description: A little-talked about investment product is taking over the 401(k) world, and offering asset managers a way to increase exposure to private markets.

item.title: Hawkish RBA Shapes as Global Outlier With Third Hike Priced In
item.description: Australia’s central bank is set to entrench its status as a hawkish outlier with a third straight interest-rate hike, diverging from peers that are mainly sitting tight to observe fallout from the US-Iran conflict.

item.title: Trump Says US to Help Some Ships Leave Hormuz Starting Monday
item.description: President Donald Trump says the US will help some ships leave Hormuz staring on Monday morning, Middle Eastern Time.

item.title: Mideast Peace Efforts in Focus as Trading Resumes: Markets Wrap
item.description: The month-long surge in risky assets faces another test as investors gauge the viability of the latest Mideast truce proposals and await a key US employment report to assess the war’s economic impact.

item.title: ECB’s Stournaras Sees Recession Concern, Phileleftheros Says
item.description: Concern about a possible euro-area recession is “real and justified” as conflict in the Middle East causes a supply-side disruption, European Central Bank Governing Council member Yannis Stournaras said in an interview with Phileleftheros.

item.title: Treasury Market on Watch for Shift in Yellen-Era Debt Playbook
item.description: Wednesday will amount to a sort of Groundhog Day for US bond dealers, who will — as has been the case for more than a year now — be watching for any change in guidance from the Treasury in its latest plan for debt issuance.

item.title: Bond Traders Look to Treasury Refunding, Fed Speakers and Jobs
item.description: US bond traders will focus on this week’s announcement of the Treasury Department’s borrowing plans for the next three months, an array of Federal Reserve speakers and a loaded calendar of economic releases crowned by monthly employment data.

item.title: Schumer Says House, White House Must Ban Prediction Market Bets
item.description: Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer called on the House of Representatives and the White House to follow the Senate’s lead by imposing a ban on trading in fast-growing prediction markets.

item.title: Bessent Says US Is ‘Suffocating’ Iran With Oil Industry Creaking
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US is “suffocating” Iran with economic and financial pressure, suggesting that the rulers in Tehran will eventually buckle.

item.title: Apple Signals New CEO Ternus Will Invest Cash Differently Than Cook
item.description: Also: The latest on iOS 27.

item.title: Private Credit Could Spark Psychological Contagion, Barr Warns
item.description: Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr said stress in private credit could spark “psychological contagion” leading to a broader credit crunch, once again warning against loosening the reins on Wall Street at a time of rising risks.

item.title: Traders Grapple With Two-Sided Tail Risk as Stocks Regain Highs
item.description: With equities back to their highs after a V-shaped recovery from the Iran oil shock, investors are increasingly caught between left and right tail risks: The tireless AI and semiconductor rally on one side and the gradual drag from higher energy prices on the other.

item.title: US Is Oil Supplier of Last Resort as Hormuz Disruptions Worsen
item.description: The tankers have been arriving from all over the world in unprecedented numbers. After loading up in Alaska and along the US Gulf Coast, they head back out to sea — to Japan, Thailand and even as far as Australia.

item.title: Big Tech Earnings Show Split Between AI Trade Winners and Losers
item.description: The world’s biggest technology companies posted strong earnings last week, showing that the artificial intelligence boom is alive and well. But in the stock market, investors are getting more granular as they try to divvy up the winners and losers in the AI trade.

item.title: ‘No Margin for Error’ Sparks Rush for Hedges in Emerging Bonds
item.description: Strategists and investors are turning to hedging and relative-value trades as a rally in emerging-market bonds looks increasingly disconnected from the looming impact of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: Argentines Keep Cash Under Mattresses Even as Milei Pushes Bank Accounts
item.description: You’d be surprised how damp and smelly dollar bills get, Alejandro Lamas says, when they’ve been squirreled away under a mattress for years. He’s gotten so many stacks of them from Argentines he’s sold used cars to over the years that he’s a bit of a connoisseur. Slip him a counterfeit and he knows at first touch.

item.title: Adnoc to Speed Growth With $55 Billion Project Awards Post-OPEC
item.description: The United Arab Emirates’ flagship oil company Adnoc is planning to accelerate a growth plan with 200 billion dirham ($55 billion) in project awards spanning upstream and downstream operations, following the country’s exit from OPEC on May 1.

item.title: OPEC+ Agrees to Symbolic June Quota Increase, Delegates Say
item.description: Major OPEC+ nations agreed a modest and symbolic increase in their June production quota levels, delegates said, as the group sends a business-as-usual message following the surprise exit of the United Arab Emirates.

item.title: HORMUZ TRACKER: Traffic Halts as Trump Weighs Iran’s New Offer
item.description: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has come to a virtual standstill while US President Donald Trump weighs Tehran’s latest peace proposal.

item.title: Major Russian Oil Export Port Primorsk Suffers Fire From UAV
item.description: Russia said Ukraine attacked Primorsk port on the Baltic Sea, a key oil export hub, causing a fire that local authorities said was quickly extinguished.

item.title: How Britain Became a Disunited Kingdom in Five Charts
item.description: Local elections this week are expected to lay bare a new reality: the two-party system that has dominated British politics since World War II has broken down.
